fact: March 27 sits exactly one week after the spring equinox, which means day and night are still battling for control but daylight is winning fast!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime time for earthworms to go absolutely bonkers - they're wiggling up from deep soil as it warms up, making birds super happy.
Cherry blossom trees in Japan are exploding with pink flowers right around now, creating what locals call 'sakura snow' when petals fall like confetti.
Why do March 27 sunrises look extra orange? The atmosphere is full of spring dust and pollen particles that scatter light in wild ways.
Hibernating animals like groundhogs and chipmunks are stumbling out of their winter hideouts around this time, groggy and confused like they just woke up from the world's longest nap.
This date falls smack in the middle of Aries season - people born now are supposedly extra fiery and headstrong, like cosmic rams charging through life.
In Antarctica, March 27 marks the beginning of serious darkness as winter approaches - penguins start huddling together in massive, warm blobs.
Maple trees are pumping out sweet sap like crazy right now in cold regions, as freezing nights and warm days create the perfect sugar rush conditions.
The moon phase around March 27 changes every year, but when it's a new moon, the night sky becomes a spectacular star-gazing playground with zero lunar interference.
Frogs and toads emerge from their muddy winter beds around this time and immediately start their loud, croaky spring concerts that can be heard from miles away.
March 27 is the 86th day of the year - that's like living through almost three full months of weird weather, holiday recovery, and winter madness.
In many places, this is when grass starts growing so fast you can practically watch it happen - some types shoot up several inches in just one week of warm weather.
